title: The Pit
content:
The thing about The Pit is that it was patient.

It did not break you all at once. It introduced you to yourself one threshold at a time, and it waited, and it watched, and it learned which levers moved which parts of you, and then it pulled them in sequence until the sequence became reflex and the reflex became you and you stopped noticing the difference.

Ember stood in the dark before the gate opened. She knew that she would be facing beasts in the arena. The keeper shoved a dagger into her hand and said simply,

"Survive and you will be rewarded."

She felt a familiar weight in her hands. She knew the feel of that hilt like it was a part of her own body. As the gates began to open the weight of survival hung on her;

*I am the last ember of everything we ever were.*

The clan had ventured long and far since she had been born, and had encountered all manner of beast the shadow plane had to offer. This first arena posed little threat to someone who was raised hunting beasts for food and survival.

The reward was warmth. Chemical and total, the sensation of having done something exactly right.

The second fight she was given the same dagger. She knew it. She had carried it for 3 years, guarding it and keeping it safe. The gate opened, and she survived. By the fourth fight she was reaching for it before they offered it. By the seventh she stopped noticing that the others were given different things each time. By the twelfth she would not have been able to tell you she'd noticed at all.

The threshold does not feel like a crossing. That is the thing about the threshold. You look back later and cannot find the exact moment your hands stopped hesitating. You can only find the moment you realized they already had.

The clan fell one at a time.

As Ember sat in her cell, awaiting what she had been told was her final fight, she looked down at her body. Once, her skin was a living canvas of the history of her clan. Adorned by intricate tattoos representing the major events of their history. Once beautiful, now torn and tattered, broken by the scars left by hundreds of fights. She wanted to weep at the thought of failure in her purpose. She was the last ember. The only record that their clan had ever existed, and those tattoos carried parts of their history, now lost. But she could not, the tears wouldn't flow.

What had she become?

Of course Cinder was last. The vampire lords were patient. They had learned which levers moved which parts of her. They knew what they were doing. Ember stood on the sand and looked across the gate at them and something in her chest performed a sound she did not make out loud. Something old and structural, like a beam giving way somewhere deep in a building that has not yet begun to fall. Her grip on the dagger tightened.

*I am the last ember of everything we ever were.*

The gates opened. Ember felt the dagger entering flesh. It was over almost before it began, as if no opposition was even offered.

That is the part that has no shape. That is the part Ember stops before, every time, and cannot enter. Not the fight. Not the end of the fight. The moment after; Ember's dagger hanging from Cinder's chest as their body began to fall limp. The expression on Cinder's face was not fear and was not hatred and was not even surprise, just;

*I know. I know it wasn't you. I forgive you.*

Cinder had been put through the same conditioning, the same patient destruction. But Cinder had carried no burden to survive. No prophecy. No purpose older than themselves. Only Ember. And Ember had not been enough.

Time stopped. Cinder's body hanging in the air between one moment and the next, and Ember looked at the dagger in their chest and remembered.

>*Firelight. The smell of something burning sweet, an herb the clan used only for ceremony.*

*Cinder's hands, steady and warm, turning the dagger over once before offering it hilt-first. The hilt they had carried since birth. Cinder's mother had pressed a fragment of her own pommel-stone into it, dark red against the pale metal. Cinder's father a sliver of worn bronze from his guard, worked flush so carefully you'd miss it if you didn't know to look.*

*Ember looked down at the dagger in her own hands. Her mother had made it for her, but the fragments came from the whole clan; every member, every dagger, every generation contributing a piece of themselves to the child who would carry them forward. She had grown up knowing what that meant. The weight of it.*

*She turned it hilt-first and extended it toward Cinder.*

*To give your dagger is to entrust your life and soul to another person. They both knew that. They had always known that.*

*Cinder, smiling. Ember's dagger already gone from her hands, already held, already theirs.*

*She took the hilt of Cinder's dagger and slid it into her sheath with a click.*
>

As time resumed, the sound of Cinder's body hitting the ground broke Ember out of that memory. The moment closed.

She reached down.

She retrieved the dagger from the body of whatever slain opponent it was lodged in.

Her hand found the hilt naturally. It always did. The gate opened.

She survived.
